articles.title: Section 271(1)(c) Penalty cannot continue after Assessee’s Death
articles.summary: The Pune Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) ruled that penalty proceedings under Section 271(1)(c) of the Income Tax Act cannot continue against the legal heirs of a deceased taxpayer. The Tribunal held that legal representatives are not personally liable for such penalties under Section 159 of the Act. This decision clarifies the scope of tax liability for heirs and has implications for tax enforcement and compliance in India.
articles.raw_summary: Pune ITAT held that penalty proceedings under Section 271(1)(c) cannot continue against legal heirs when initiated after the assessee’s demise. The Tribunal ruled that legal representatives are not personally liable for such penalty proceedings under Section 159. The post Section 271(1)(c) Penalty cannot continue after Assessee’s Death appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: ITAT Deletes ₹9.75 Cr Addition as Cash Deposits Were Explained Through Business Receipts
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Rajkot deleted a ₹9.75 crore addition made by tax authorities regarding unexplained cash deposits during demonetization. The Tribunal ruled that the deposits were fully supported by audited books, cash books, and bank records, and that additions under Section 69A cannot be sustained merely on suspicion. This decision reinforces the legal requirement for evidence-based tax assessments and may influence future tax enforcement practices.

articles.title: NCLT Directs Liquidation After 741-Day CIRP Expires Without Approved Resolution Plan
articles.summary: The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) has ordered the liquidation of a company after its Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) exceeded 741 days without an approved resolution plan. The CIRP expired on 20 November 2025, and since no plan was approved by the Committee of Creditors (CoC), the tribunal invoked Section 33 of the IBC to initiate liquidation. This highlights delays in India's insolvency resolution process and the legal consequences of prolonged CIRP periods.
articles.raw_summary: Tribunal noted that the CIRP period, including all extensions, had reached 741 days and expired on 20 November 2025. Since no plan was approved by the CoC, liquidation under Section 33 of the IBC was ordered. The post NCLT Directs Liquidation After 741-Day CIRP Expires Without Approved Resolution Plan appeared first on TaxGuru .

The verdict is a legal action with implications for gender rights and welfare policies in India.
articles.raw_summary: Both are practices shaped by patriarchy and religion. But one is determined by individual autonomy and the other by biology.

articles.title: RBI rate hikes to start in June, says Standard Chartered

articles.title: NCLAT refuses to condone 1-day delay beyond statutory 45-day limit under Section 61(2) IBC despite e-filing portal glitches
articles.summary: The National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) refused to condone a one-day delay in filing beyond the statutory 45-day limit under Section 61(2) of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC), even due to technical glitches in the e-filing portal. The tribunal emphasized that it cannot exceed its jurisdiction as defined by the legislature, stating that granting such condonation would undermine legislative intent. The ruling underscores the strict adherence to statutory timelines in insolvency
articles.raw_summary: NCLAT observed that Tribunal cannot clothe itself with a jurisdiction which has not been vested upon it by the legislature. Thus, grant condonation of delay beyond the 15th day even on equitable consideration or on the ground of hardship or technical considerations will amount to jettisoning the intent of the legislature which is impermissible. The post NCLAT refuses to condone 1-day delay beyond statutory 45-day limit under Section 61(2) IBC despite e-filing portal glitches appeared first on SC

articles.title: Section 148 Notice Invalid as It Was Issued by JAO Instead of FAO: Rajasthan HC
articles.summary: The Rajasthan High Court quashed a reassessment notice issued under Section 148 of the Income Tax Act, ruling it invalid because it was issued by a Jurisdictional Assessing Officer (JAO) instead of the Faceless Assessing Officer (FAO) as required. The court held that notices under Section 148 must follow the faceless mechanism under Section 151A and the 2022 Scheme. This decision underscores the strict procedural compliance required for tax reassessments in India.

This decision reinforces the need for thorough scrutiny of expense claims in tax assessments.

articles.title: ITAT Allows Section 54 Deduction as Delay in Sale Deed Registration Was Beyond Assessee’s Control
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) ruled that a delay in registering a sale deed beyond the assessee's control does not invalidate their claim for a Section 54 tax deduction. The tribunal accepted that full payment, TDS deduction, and possession transfer established the transaction's completion for capital gains purposes, directing taxation for Assessment Year 2018-19 and allowing the deduction. The ruling clarifies tax compliance for property transactions where registration delays are una
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal found that full payment, TDS deduction, and transfer of possession established completion of the transaction for capital gains purposes. It therefore directed taxation in AY 2018-19 and allowed the Section 54 claim. articles.summary: The Pune Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) restored a Section 12AA registration matter after a trust claimed it had no knowledge of notices issued by the Commissioner of Income Tax (Exemption). The case highlights procedural fairness in tax administration and the importance of due process for trusts seeking tax exemptions.

articles.title: Fragmented Producer Responsibility Rules Are Raising Data and Traceability Expectations for Apparel and Textile Brands
articles.summary: Producer responsibility rules for textiles and apparel are expanding globally, requiring brands to track product-level data and ensure traceability. A policy analysis by Cascale and Worldly highlights these obligations across Europe, Asia, and the Americas. For Indian textile brands and manufacturers, this could mean adapting to stricter compliance standards and interoperability demands. The shift reflects broader environmental and regulatory trends impacting the industry.
articles.raw_summary: Producer responsibility schemes for textiles and apparel are expanding across major markets, bringing new obligations for product-level data, traceability, and interoperability. A policy deep dive from Cascale and Worldly maps the regulatory landscape across Europe, Asia, and the Americas, outlining practical implications for brands and manufacturers as compliance complexity continues to grow.

articles.title: ITAT Indore Sets Aside Ex Parte CIT(A) Order as Notices Were Sent to Wrong Email Address
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Indore overturned an ex parte order passed by the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) because key hearing notices were sent to an incorrect email address. The tribunal ruled that this violated the principles of natural justice, as the assessee was not given a fair opportunity to present their case. The matter has been remanded back for fresh adjudication. The decision highlights the importance of proper procedural compliance in tax appeals.
articles.raw_summary: ITAT Indore held that appellate order violated principles of natural justice after finding that key hearing notices were sent to an incorrect email address. The matter was remanded for fresh adjudication. The post ITAT Indore Sets Aside Ex Parte CIT(A) Order as Notices Were Sent to Wrong Email Address appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: 2026 LiveLaw (SC) 534 | Mallika v. R. Nallathambi & Ors
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that plaintiffs must prove that General Power of Attorney (GPA) transactions were secured as loans, not outright sales, and that mere allegations of fraud are insufficient. articles.title: SC refers matter of bail in UAPA cases to larger bench amid difference of opinion on Umar Khalid bail
articles.summary: The Supreme Court, led by Justice Aravind Kumar, referred a matter regarding bail in UAPA (Unlawful Activities Prevention Act) cases to a larger bench due to differing opinions on granting bail to accused individuals facing prolonged incarceration. The court questioned whether the K.A. Najeeb judgment, which allows constitutional courts to grant bail in such cases, is an absolute directive. articles.title: Govt relaxes insolvency rules for MSMEs
articles.summary: The Indian government has relaxed insolvency rules for Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) to simplify the process. Starting in 2026, only one valuer will be appointed per asset class to reduce costs, and strict new regulations will prevent conflicts of interest in pre-packaged resolutions. Related parties, recent auditors, and associates of resolution professionals are now barred from appointment.
articles.raw_summary: New rules aim to simplify insolvency for MSMEs. Only one valuer will be appointed per asset class, cutting costs. Strict new regulations prevent conflicts of interest in pre-packaged resolutions. Related parties, recent auditors, and associates of resolution professionals are now barred from appointment. These changes are effective from 2026.

articles.title: CCI Rejects Excessive Pricing Allegations Against Super-Specialty Hospital for Lack of Comparable Evidence
articles.summary: The Competition Commission of India (CCI) dismissed allegations of excessive pricing against a private super-specialty hospital, stating there was insufficient evidence to prove the pricing was both excessive and unfair. The hospital had been accused of charging higher prices for tests, medicines, and consumables. The CCI's decision highlights the need for comparable evidence in such cases. This ruling sets a precedent for future competition law enforcement in the healthcare sector.
articles.raw_summary: The CCI held that higher prices charged by a private super-specialty hospital for tests, medicines, and consumables did not automatically amount to abuse of dominance. The Commission found no sufficient evidence proving that the pricing was both excessive and unfair. The post CCI Rejects Excessive Pricing Allegations Against Super-Specialty Hospital for Lack of Comparable Evidence appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.summary: GST-registered businesses in India can now digitally record the completion of goods delivery on the e-way bill portal, a voluntary feature introduced by GSTN. Previously, the portal only tracked goods movement without a formal closure record. This change aims to strengthen transaction trails, improve data integrity, and enhance accountability in GST compliance. Experts suggest it may eventually become mandatory to curb malpractices and aid reconciliation.

This new voluntary feature on the GST portal strengthens transaction trails and accountability. Previously, e-way bills tracked goods movement but lacked a formal completion record. This move aims to improve data integrity and traceability. Experts suggest this could become mandatory, enhancing reconciliation and curbing malpractices.

articles.title: Two judgments, no single standard: Why terror law UAPA’s bail threshold isn’t a straight line
articles.summary: The Supreme Court has delivered conflicting judgments on bail provisions under India's stringent anti-terror law, the UAPA. One ruling emphasizes strict conditions for bail, while another prioritizes individual liberty. This contradiction has reignited debates over which legal standard should govern UAPA cases, with implications for fundamental rights and judicial consistency in terror-related prosecutions.
articles.raw_summary: SC’s contradiction over bail in terror cases has revived questions over whether the stringent Watali test or the liberty-focused Najeeb ruling should guide UAPA jurisprudence.

The issue is a hurdle for the India-UK economic pact.

articles.title: AI Promises Efficiency but the Fashion Industry Needs a Political Reckoning
articles.summary: Fashion for Good's report outlines a vision for an AI-driven fashion supply chain by 2036, emphasizing technical feasibility and operational seriousness. However, it raises unaddressed concerns about labour shifts, legal responsibility, and regulatory gaps that could either drive progress or perpetuate existing systemic failures. The discussion underscores the need for policy frameworks to manage AI's societal and economic impacts in industries like fashion.
articles.raw_summary: Ten years is a long horizon in any industry, but in AI it may be a category error. Fashion for Good's mapping of a 2036 AI-native supply chain is technically grounded and operationally serious. The structural questions it leaves unanswered, around rebound effects, labour transition, legal accountability, and regulatory reach, are the ones that will determine whether the vision it describes produces genuine progress or faster versions of the same systemic failures.

This highlights the importance of the census as a governance tool while addressing data privacy concerns.

articles.title: 'Child Marriages On Rise In UP': Allahabad HC Blames UP Police Failure To Book Grooms, Facilitators Under PCMA
articles.summary: The Allahabad High Court highlighted a rise in child marriages in Uttar Pradesh and criticized the state police for failing to prosecute grooms and facilitators under the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act (PCMA), 2006. The court clarified that the secular PCMA prevails over personal laws like the Hindu Marriage Act and Muslim Marriages and Divorce Act. This ruling underscores systemic gaps in enforcement and the need for stronger measures to protect children.
articles.raw_summary: The Court also clarified that, being a secular Act, 2006 Act prevails over provisions of Hindu Marriage Act & Muslim Marriages and Divorce Act.

articles.title: Entire Bogus Purchase Cannot Be Added When Sales Are Not Disputed: ITAT Rajkot
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Rajkot ruled that in cases involving bogus purchases, only the embedded profit element (13.7%) can be added to taxable income, not the full purchase amount, when sales are not disputed. This decision impacts how tax authorities assess income in such cases. The ruling clarifies the scope of additions for bogus transactions under tax law.
articles.raw_summary: ITAT Rajkot held that in cases involving bogus purchases, only the profit element embedded in such purchases can be added to income. The Tribunal upheld restriction of the addition to 13.7% instead of the full purchase amount. The post Entire Bogus Purchase Cannot Be Added When Sales Are Not Disputed: ITAT Rajkot appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Air Ticket Booking Alone Not Taxable as ‘Tour Operator’ Service: CESTAT Chennai
articles.summary: CESTAT Chennai held that mere trading in air tickets did not amount to taxable “Tour Operator” service. The Tribunal ruled that retaining discounts on ticket sales was outside the scope of service tax. The post Air Ticket Booking Alone Not Taxable as ‘Tour Operator’ Service: CESTAT Chennai appeared first on TaxGuru .

The move aims to safeguard agricultural output amid potential climate challenges during the kharif season.

articles.title: ETtech Explainer: How Swiggy’s failed bid to become an Indian firm matters for Instamart
articles.summary: Swiggy's bid to become an Indian-owned-and-controlled company (IOCC) has been delayed after shareholders failed to approve changes to its articles of association.
